metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Color system

header.title: Color system
header.description: A set of colors with designated roles to create a cohesive and accessible visual identity.

sections.text:
What is a color system?

A color system is a structured approach to using color within a design project. It goes beyond just providing a collection of colors—it defines how those colors work together to create a cohesive and purposeful visual identity.

Atmosphere's color system

In addition to solid white (#FFFFFF) and solid black (#000000), Atmosphere's color system has three main palette groups:

  • Brand solids
  • Neutral solids
  • Extended solids

These palettes are the foundation for colors in the base token structure. All Atmosphere components and data palettes use alias tokens that reference this color system.

General concepts

Base color token

Base color tokens are the fundamental building blocks of a color palette. Atmosphere's base token library ensures that the design system is scalable, especially as use cases for new colors arise.

Alias color token

To help you select the right color, alias tokens have a label that describes the intended use of the color. For example, the alias token for error-message borders is called foundation.semantic.border.error instead of the color code #FF5261.

Alias tokens can contain words that refer to different roles and behaviors, including:

  • Accent - Emphasizes important elements with distinguished brand colors
  • Background - Sets the page and component background color
  • Glass - Mimics the depth of translucent material
  • Glow - Highlights interactive states on small surfaces in dark mode
  • High, medium, low - Defines emphasis through color
  • Interactive - Encourages action within an element or component
  • Inverse - Switches an element to its light or dark mode variant
  • Semantic - Assign meaning to certain elements
  • Static - Keeps an element the same in both dark and light mode
  • Text and icon - Sets colors for text or icon elements only

images.text: To make sure that your design meets expected visual and accessibility guidelines, always use Atmosphere's default component tokens
Never change or customize Atmosphere's default component tokens, as this leads to improper color mappings and produces unintended visual results

sections.text:
Dark and light mode

Design for light and dark modes

The Atmosphere color palette has variants for light and dark mode, making it easy for you to swap between color modes in your designs.

Light and dark mode have subtle style differences. For example, dark mode has a glow effect that works well on dark elements.

Always consider user comfort and readability

Light mode

  • To ensure legibility, prioritize high contrast between text and background, especially in well-lit environments
  • Ensure sufficient contrast for accessibility, based on WCAG guidelines

Dark mode

  • Aim for comfortable viewing in low-light conditions
  • Use a dark background with lighter text colors that have sufficient contrast

Changing color modes

To ensure a seamless transition between themes, all alias color tokens are mapped to both light and dark mode. By default, the Atmosphere component library is set to the dark mode theme.

To switch the theme, change the color mode at page level.

Atmosphere strategy

The success of Atmosphere lies in creating a flexible, adaptable system with strong governance and documentation that enables teams to build quickly and efficiently. Atmosphere is managed as a product and is constantly refined based on user data and feedback, making sure that it serves the current needs of DAS and remains future-proof. With a strong focus on modularity, (AI) automation, collaboration, and continuous improvements, Atmosphere will drive both the growth and quality of our digital products while keeping teams aligned and productive.
items: Review the alias tokens to learn more about their intended roles
Add the Foundations and Components Librarywhen you create new files
Submit a design requestif you identify a need for a new color token

sections.text:
UI color tokens

UI color tokens are the foundation of all user interfaces built with Atmosphere. These color tokens are based on the Atmosphere neutral solids palette, and were crafted to optimize accessibility.

Use UI color tokens for all text, icons, backgrounds, and borders of non-interactive UI elements.

Background

Background tokens are applied to larger surfaces, such as containers and component surfaces.

For page backgrounds, use the following token: foundation.ui.background.page. This ensures that the layering in all apps have the same starting point, which helps us to maintain consistency and create a cohesive visual identity across our applications.

You can apply three emphasis tokens to surfaces: high, medium, or low. Select the emphasis token that best meets the level of contrast needed with the background.
sections.imgText: Left to right: page background token and high, medium, low emphasis surfaces

sections.text:
Glass background

Glass tokens mimic the depth of a translucent material and are particularly powerful on backgrounds without solid colors such as maps.

In Atmosphere, glass tokens are applied sparingly to different types of surfaces. The following components have been designed with glass tokens:

  • Navigation bar
  • Top-app bar
  • Form helpers
  • Cards
  • Cards
  • Dialogs
  • Snackbars

Depending on the translucency level needed for the page background, you can choose from five emphasis tokens to glass surfaces: thickest, thick, regular, thin, thinnest.
sections.imgText: Example glass tokens: thin (top) and thick (right)

sections.text:
Text and icon

For non-interactive text and icons, such as headers, titles, bodies of text, or illustrative icons, use text and icon color tokens.

Depending on the level of contrast or hierarchy needed in the user interface, you can use three different emphasis tokens: high, medium, or low.
sections.imgText: Text and icon tokens applied with high, medium, and low emphasis

sections.text:
Interactive

Use interactive color tokens for elements that should change states upon user action, such as icons, buttons, and links.

Interactive tokens apply to the text, icons, backgrounds, state layers, and borders of interactive UI elements.

Primary

Primary interactive tokens are the default tokens for interactive components. These color tokens apply to borders, text, icon, state fill colors, and background glow.

Accent

To help enforce branding in the UI, you can use accent interactive tokens. Components that use these tokens stand out because they use the Flight brand solids palette, which contains distinct blue hues. Primary tokens use the more neutral Space brand solids palette.

Only some Atmosphere components include accent tokens, such as common, icon, and FAB buttons.

To avoid cluttering the UI and avoid distracting users, use accent tokens only for a few select use cases. For example, to highlight page-level interactions.

Static

Static color tokens have the same color value in both light and dark mode.

Use static tokens if the color should stay the same in both color modes.

sections.imgAlt: example image of static color tokens

sections.text:
Semantic

Semantic color tokens help to convey a message through color. Consistent use of semantic tokens help users who can perceive color to derive meaning from the UI at a glance.

You can apply semantic tokens to borders, text, icon, and fill colors.

sections.imgAlt: example image of semantic token usage

sections.text:
Atmosphere's semantic palette highlights the state of items that are part of a workflow. In general, these colors inform the user of the workflow status. They can also prompt the user to take action if needed.

Atmosphere's semantic palette highlights the state of items that are part of a workflow. In general, these colors inform the user of the workflow status. They can also prompt the user to take action if needed.

Neutral

The default state that shows the item is part of a workflow. Use if the item doesn’t require (immediate) action, or for neutral statuses that don’t block the user’s flow.

Success

Use when something desirable has happened.

Information

Use to indicate a state of active progress that doesn't require (immediate) user action.

Warning

Use when the user might experience a negative impact if they don't take action, to help them prevent problems from happening.

Error

Use when something is problematic and requires (immediate) user action. Use sparingly, as these statuses might be stressful to the user.

images.text: Never apply your own semantic colors
Don't modify default semantic colors, unless specified in the component

sections.text:
UI background

Atmosphere has three types of background:

Page background

Apply to the lowest layer of an application using the dedicated token: foundation.ui.background.page

Flat background

Apply to large surfaces, such as containers. Depending on the level of contrast needed with the background, you can use high, medium, or low emphasis tokens.

Glass background

Apply to large surfaces such as containers, especially if the background doesn't use a solid color. Depending on the level of translucency needed with the background, you can use thickest, thick, regular, thin, or thinnest emphasis tokens.
sections.imgText: Layering example with page background (1), flat backgrounds (2,  5, 6) and glass backgrounds (3, 4)

sections.text:
Layering surfaces

To create depth and spatial associations, layer surfaces on top of each other. The foundation for layering surfaces is always the page background color.

By default, you should place all content inside a container. Never change default component background colors, as appropriate layering has been built directly into all Atmosphere themes, color tokens, and components.

Layering surfaces

To create depth and spatial associations, layer surfaces on top of each other. The foundation for layering surfaces is always the page background color.

By default, you should place all content inside a container. Never change default component background colors, as appropriate layering has been built directly into all Atmosphere themes, color tokens, and components.

images.text: To ensure proper application layering and accessibility, always use Atmosphere's default page background token
Never use other background colors for the page because this negatively affects the layering of all components in the interface

sections.text:
What is glass material?

Glass effects mimic the appearance of frosted or translucent glass. Content appears to float over a blurred background with a semi-transparent or frosted glass-like look, which has numerous benefits:

  • Gives a sense of depth to a design and a feeling of visual hierarchy
  • Helps to keep the focus on foreground elements while still letting users perceive the background
  • Creates a modern, polished experience

How is the glass effect built?

To achieve the glass effect, assign these four characteristics to an empty container:

Glass material

Provides depth to the container, establishing layering between different elements. Depending on the contrast needed, you can choose from five emphasis options: thickest, thick, regular, thin, or thinnest.

Glass border specular

Gives the container a glass-like specular effect along its borders.

Glass surface specular

Adds a specular effect to the surface of the glass. Mostly applied to larger surfaces.

Glass translucency

Adds a semi-transparent quality to the glass.

When to use glass material

Some Atmosphere components have a glass effect by default, such as the top app bar, navigation drawer, snackbar, and form fields. Several drop-down elements also contain glass backgrounds.

Never change default glass component backgrounds.

Other surface components, such as cards and side sheets, let you choose between a glass or a flat background.

Before you apply a glass background, make sure that it's suitable for your use case.

Use cases

Before using a glass effect, carefully analyze whether it fits your use case.

If the application is data-heavy and requires a lot of data analysis or user comprehension, glass material might add to the user's extraneous cognitive load.

Glass material is better suited for minimal and lightweight user interfaces, or for those where the background is an essential part of the experience.

Performance

Glass material is harder to render than a solid background, which might result in a different performance of your application.

To optimize performance, use glass material sparingly.
sections.imgAlt: Example image of glass and flat backgrounds
sections.imgText: Some components provide a choice between glass and flat background

sections.text:
Contrast and accessibility

Text used in glass material elements is optimized to have a high or medium contrast with the background.

If you use glass material on a page or a low, medium, or high background, it complies with the minimum 4.5:1 ratio specified by WCAG.

If you use glass material on a custom background, such as a gradient, an image, or a map, accessibility isn't guaranteed.

Always check accessibility if you use glass material over a custom background.
sections.imgAlt: Example image of using glass on a custom background
sections.imgText: Check the accessibility of glass material on a custom background.

items: Use pre-built components to ensure accessibility
Check custom color contrasts with accessibility checker tools

sections.text:
Color and Accessibility

People perceive color in different ways, which you need to take into account when you design with color. When you design with color vision deficiencies in mind, you might check color combinations to make sure that UI elements don't blend into each other.

Opacity and weight also have a powerful impact on how color is perceived. For example, low opacity results in lighter colors, which can make text illegible. People who are nearsighted struggle to read text that's set to 30% opacity, even from a reasonable distance.

Color contrast

Color contrast is calculated as a ratio, based on the difference between the luminance of background and foreground elements. For example, to check whether button text is legible, you can measure the contrast between the button label and surface colors. If you check designs in grayscale, you can also check the hierarchy and contrast of different colors.

To check the accessibility of your design, you can use Figma plugins such as Stark Contrast & Accessibility Checker or A11y Color Contrast Checker.

Keep in mind that accessibility tools also have limitations. Ultimately, you should use your good judgment and test designs with users. You should also consider needs specific to your user's context that might require a high or reduced contrast mode.

Contrast ratios

The contrast ratio is the difference between colors that live on top of or next to one another, also known as adjacent colors. A good contrast ratio helps users to perceive and understand the interface. If a design element contains information that's critical to understanding the interaction, it must comply with the following WCAG contrast ratios:

  • 4.5:1 or higher - Non-objects, such as normal text. When used on a solid or glass background, Atmosphere's high and medium tokens fully comply with this contrast ratio.
  • 3:1 or higher - Objects and large text, such as buttons, 18 point or higher text, or 14 point bold text. When used on a solid or glass background, Atmosphere's high, medium, and low tokens fully comply with this contrast ratio.

Accent and contrast ratio

In light mode, accent colors don't pass the contrast ratio requirements when used in components that contain text, such as buttons or links. However, the Atmosphere team understands the need to keep an accent blue as part of the Boeing brand.

You can use the accent palette at your own discretion. It's your responsibility to make sure that your design is accessible to your users.
